title: 18.-Authorization to Encrypt Berkeley Police Department Primary Radio Channels

sections.recommendation:
Adopt a Resolution rescinding the City Council’s existing policy (established in
September of 2021) that limits the Police Department’s use of encrypted public safety
radio transmissions to Special Response Team operations, special patrol and
investigative operations, prolonged critical incidents, execution of search warrants, or
transmissions of Criminal Justice Information (CJI) and Personally Identifiable
Information (PII) pursuant to California Department of Justice (DOJ) Bulletin 20-09-CJIS
and authorize the encryption of all primary Berkeley Police Department (BPD) radio
channels.
SUMMARY
This report requests Council approval to rescind the 2021 directive keeping most BPD
radio channels unencrypted and to authorize encryption of all primary dispatch
channels. The action will align Berkeley with every other Alameda and Contra Costa
County agency that will transition to encryption in October 2025, ensure compliance
with California DOJ and federal CJIS security requirements, protect community privacy,
and address well-documented officer safety and operational integrity concerns.
sections.fiscal_impacts:
The City has already procured Motorola radios with built-in encryption capability. No
additional costs are anticipated and, if necessary, any training or administrative costs
associated with the transition will be absorbed within the Police Department’s existing
operating budget.
sections.current_situation:
The Berkeley Police Department’s primary and secondary dispatch channels currently
remain unencrypted and therefore accessible in real time via scanner applications and
internet streaming services. In recent months and over the last several years, BPD
officers and detectives have documented multiple incidents where suspects actively
monitored unencrypted radio traffic while committing crimes, most often in coordinated

overnight commercial burglaries involving multiple suspects. Intelligence shared through
regional partnerships confirms that this is widespread, organized, and in some cases
involves armed offenders.
Local examples include arrests in which suspects were stopped while listening to BPD’s
primary channel on a mobile device, recovery of phones from suspects that were
actively broadcasting BPD channels, and investigative video showing lookouts using
hand-to-ear signals consistent with monitoring radio traffic and alerting accomplices
immediately prior to officer arrival. These behaviors demonstrate that suspects use
scanner apps and similar tools to evade capture, to organize sideshows and other mass
gatherings, and to track officer movements in real time.
The officer safety implications are substantial. When suspects have advance notice of
officer presence or activity in an area, it not only enables their evasion of arrest; it can
create a tactical disadvantage for responding personnel and increases the risk of a
violent encounter. Advance knowledge of officer locations, response times, and tactics
allows offenders to avoid detection, to reposition or withdraw, to prepare aggressive
responses, or to set up ambushes if a confrontation ensues. The risk is greatest during

sections.background:
BPD is a member of the East Bay Regional Communications System Authority, a
shared radio system designed to provide interoperable communications for police, fire,
and other responders across Alameda and Contra Costa Counties. On October 12,
2020, the California Department of Justice issued Information Bulletin 20-09-CJIS,
which requires the protection of Criminal Justice Information (CJI) and Personally
Identifiable Information (PII) transmitted over radio systems and directs agencies to use
encryption consistent with the FBI CJIS Security Policy. Agencies unable to encrypt
immediately were directed to adopt restrictive interim workarounds and submit
compliance plans with the understanding that full encryption would be implemented over
time.
In September 2021, Council considered encryption as part of approving the City’s
Motorola radio contract. At that time, the County was not yet prepared to implement
systemwide encryption and Council adopted a written policy that, except for Special
Response Team operations and transmissions of CJI or PII pursuant to DOJ Bulletin
20-09-CJIS, most public safety radio transmissions remain unencrypted and accessible
to the media and public through scanners or similar technology. The City’s Motorola
system was procured with encryption capability and the Council’s action recognized
encryption would eventually become necessary.
EBRCSA has since finalized a systemwide transition to AES 256-bit encryption for
October of this year. Without Council approval to move forward with encryption,
Berkeley will be the only agency in either county whose main dispatch radio
communications remain publicly accessible in real time.
ENVIRONMENTAL SUSTAINABILITY AND CLIMATE IMPACTS
There are no identifiable environmental sustainability or climate impacts associated with
this action.
sections.rationale:
Operating unencrypted radio channels is inconsistent with DOJ directives and exposes
individuals, officers, and the City to significant risk. This is due to the operational reality
that responding to and investigating calls for service regularly requires transmitting
names, dates of birth, driver’s license numbers, warrant information, addresses, or
mental health information. As neighboring cities and counties move to protect CJI and
PII, Berkeley risks being out of step with privacy standards and regulatory expectations.
More critically, continuing to broadcast sensitive information without encryption creates
tangible vulnerabilities that criminals exploit to avoid capture, to harm officers or
community members, and to jeopardize ongoing investigations and prosecutions. While
interim workarounds such as encrypted secondary channels and mobile data terminals
have been used, those measures are operationally inefficient and unsustainable given
staffing constraints in sworn and dispatch personnel. Continued reliance on these

Authorization to Encrypt Berkeley Police Department
Primary Radio Channels

October 28, 2025
workarounds increases the likelihood of delayed or incomplete information, reduced
situational awareness, and heightened safety risks. To maintain transparency and
media access, the Berkeley Fire Department’s radio channels will remain unencrypted,
ensuring continued public awareness of major incidents such as fires, collisions, and
other critical events that often involve joint police and fire responses. This is possible as
the fire channels do not typically include PII or police tactics.
Encrypting BPD’s primary radio channels will protect officer safety and operational
integrity by preventing suspects from anticipating or countering police response, and by
eliminating the tactical advantage criminals currently gain from open access to dispatch
communications. Encryption will safeguard community privacy by ensuring that
sections.alternatives:
One alternative is to maintain the current policy and continue using interim workarounds
such as encrypted secondary channels and mobile data computers. This approach was
rejected because it is operationally inefficient, burdens personnel, does not fully meet
DOJ/CJIS expectations, and leaves the City exposed to increased risk. A second
alternative is to delay implementation of encryption; this was rejected because it would
leave Berkeley as the sole unencrypted agency in the region, concentrate criminal
attention on our open channels, and increase potential liability and safety risks.
CONTACT PERSON
Jennifer Louis, Chief of Police, Police Department, (510) 981-5700

RESOLUTION NO. ##,###-N.S.
RESCINDING THE CITY COUNCIL’S POLICY REGARDING UNENCRYPTED PUBLIC
SAFETY RADIO CHANNELS AND AUTHORIZING THE ENCRYPTION OF BERKELEY
POLICE DEPARTMENT PRIMARY RADIO CHANNELS
WHEREAS, the California Department of Justice issued Information Bulletin 20-09-CJIS
(October 12, 2020) requiring encryption of all Criminal Justice Information (CJI) and
Personally Identifiable Information (PII) transmitted over radio systems, consistent with
the FBI CJIS Security Policy; and
WHEREAS, in September 2021 the City Council adopted a policy that most City public
safety radio transmissions remain unencrypted, except for Special Response Team
operations and transmissions of CJI/PII; and
WHEREAS, the Berkeley Police Department has documented numerous incidents in
which suspects used unencrypted radio traffic to coordinate crimes, evade capture, and
endanger officers and community members; and
WHEREAS, advance notice of officer presence or activity creates a tactical disadvantage
and increases the risk of violent confrontation or ambush; and
WHEREAS, failure to encrypt would leave Berkeley the only unencrypted agency in
Alameda and Contra Costa Counties, creating a unique public safety risk, undermining
regional consistency, and exposing the City to liability; and
WHEREAS, the East Bay Regional Communications System Authority (EBRCSA) has
scheduled encryption for Contra Costa agencies on October 7, 2025, and Alameda
County agencies, including Berkeley, on October 15, 2025, which is the earliest date BPD
could transition; however, actual implementation will occur as soon as practicable
following Council authorization and administrative preparation.
NOW THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED by the Council of the City of Berkeley that the
policy adopted on September 28, 2021, of transmitting unencrypted information on
primary channels is hereby resc
